claim: Toolkit bandwidth at precisely 155-240bp calculated via multiplicative formula (500bp x 0.6 x 0.7 x 0.85 = 180bp)
reason: Conjunction fallacy: assumes independence of constraints that share common causes. Direction survives (bandwidth substantially less than 500bp, likely less than half) but the specific range does not.

claim: BoJ normalization is the single most underpriced exogenous shock to UST markets
reason: Own quantification at 30% execution yields only 10-15bp (within noise). Thesis consensus since 2023 (definitionally not underpriced). July 2024 unwind absorbed in three weeks. 2006-07 normalization did not trigger predicted repatriation.

claim: Natural gas storage surplus as evidence of broken monetary transmission
reason: ~40-45% of US dry gas is Permian associated gas, insensitive to gas prices or interest rates. No plausible transmission channel from FFR to associated gas output. Gas is insensitive to monetary policy, not evidence that transmission is broken.

claim: BoJ normalization triggers 200-400 kb/d Japanese refinery rationalization with inflationary impact
reason: Margin compression only $0.50-1.00/bbl (within normal volatility). Japanese closures are government-managed at ~100-150 kb/d per decade. Claimed inflationary pressure from ~1% global capacity reduction is vanishingly small.

claim: 2s10s steepening is approximately 70% term premium driven
reason: Assumes parallel expectations shifts across the curve, which is demonstrably false given dot plot dispersion and bimodal terminal rate pricing. Direction likely correct but specific 70% magnitude is not derivable.

claim: $700-900B in three reinforcing structural USD demand flows
reason: Capex dollar equity inflows and cloud invoicing are not independent — equity inflows fund the companies whose revenue creates the invoicing channel. Overstates net new USD demand by ~20-30% due to double-counting.

claim: Credit-equity basis may persist indefinitely due to private credit growth
reason: Requires assuming infinite private credit capacity. Maturity wall will force borrowers to public markets if private credit capacity exhausts. More persistent than historically, but not indefinite.
